query: A calibration instrument has a long-established population standard deviation from years of operation. A fresh sample of 64 readings gives an average slightly above the target. The engineer asks for a two-sided uncertainty interval for the true center of the process.

Historical production records fix the measurement spread at 3.2 units. A random batch of 49 items has a sample mean of 101.6. Give the interval estimate for the unknown process mean, not for a future observation.

The noise level of a sensor is specified by the manufacturer and may be treated as known. After 100 independent measurements, the average response is recorded. Construct a 95% interval for the underlying mean response.

Bolt diameters have a known process standard deviation from a controlled machine study. A small new sample gives an average diameter. The question asks for an interval around the population mean diameter.

The spread of individual readings is not estimated from the current data; it is supplied externally. The only unknown is the center of the distribution, and the task is a two-sided confidence interval.
A long-run sigma is accepted for a stable process. A fresh run of 81 observations produces an average, and the report needs the confidence limits for the true process mean.
A quality laboratory uses a certified population standard deviation. The sample mean from 36 observations is to be converted into a confidence interval using the appropriate normal critical value.
The problem gives both a sample average and a historical population standard deviation. The sample standard deviation is not requested. Identify the interval method for the true mean.

Only the current sample of 12 observations is available, and the population spread is not known. The problem states that the measurements may be treated as normal and asks for a confidence interval for the mean.
A sample mean and a sample standard deviation are reported from 18 specimens. No historical sigma is available. Construct an interval estimate for the population mean.
Material strength is assumed normally distributed. From 15 specimens the sample average and sample standard deviation are obtained. The task is to quantify uncertainty in the true mean strength.
The current experiment estimates both the center and the spread. The question asks for a two-sided interval for the population center, and the sample size is not large.
Nine lab readings are approximately normal. Their average and sample standard deviation are given. Find an interval for the true average reading.
A manufacturer has no reliable previous estimate of process standard deviation. A random sample provides xbar and s. The required output is a confidence interval for mu.
A small normal sample is summarized by its mean and standard deviation. Decide the correct confidence interval procedure and state the distributional assumption behind it.
Fifty-one aerospace parts are sampled and the sample standard deviation is reported. Assuming normality, construct a two-sided confidence interval for the population standard deviation.
A normal process is sampled, and the engineer wants a confidence interval for the population variance itself rather than the mean.
Repeated measurements from a precision instrument appear normal. The task is not to test a claim but to estimate the uncertainty range for the true standard deviation.
A sample variance from n independent normal observations is given. Use the sampling distribution of the scaled sample variance to form confidence limits for sigma squared.
The problem asks for an interval estimate of the process spread and explicitly warns that the chi-square procedure depends on normality.
A quality report needs lower and upper confidence bounds for sigma based on a sample standard deviation from normally distributed measurements.
Out of a large random sample of shipped items, a certain number are defective. Construct an approximate confidence interval for the true defective proportion.
A survey records how many respondents support a design change. The required output is a confidence interval for the population proportion, not a hypothesis test.
The data are binary pass/fail outcomes from many independent trials. Estimate the unknown success probability with a two-sided interval.
A binomial count is summarized by x successes in n trials, with both n p-hat and n(1-p-hat) large enough. The question asks for uncertainty in p.
Only one group is observed, and the quantity of interest is a rate of occurrence expressed as a fraction. Provide an interval estimate for that rate.
Two independent production lines are sampled, and long-run standard deviations for both lines are treated as known. Construct a confidence interval for mu1 minus mu2.
Two unrelated groups have noticeably different sample variances and unequal sample sizes. The task is to estimate the difference in population means.
Two independent normal samples are taken under conditions where equal population variances are assumed. The question asks for an interval for the difference of means.
The same response is measured in two separate groups of specimens. There is no pairing. Find an interval for the treatment mean minus the control mean.
Two formulations are tested on independent specimens, and the process standard deviation is known from prior experience. Estimate the difference in true mean responses.
Each specimen is measured once under method A and once under method B. The report asks for a confidence interval for the mean paired difference.
The same subjects are measured before and after a procedure. The analysis should quantify uncertainty in the average within-subject change.
Ten matched units are tested under two conditions. The raw observations come in pairs, and the desired interval is for the mean difference within pairs.
Each worker performs both procedures on different days. Construct an interval for the average increase caused by the new procedure.

A report says a 95% interval for mu has been obtained. The question asks which interpretation is correct and rejects the statement that there is a 95% probability that the fixed mu lies in this particular computed interval.
Two proposed confidence intervals for the same mean use different confidence levels and sample sizes. Identify which factors make the interval wider or narrower.
A regression problem asks whether the uncertainty band is for the average response at x0 or for one new future observation at x0.
The task asks why a t critical value appears in a mean interval when sigma is estimated from the same normal sample.
A normal population with known variance is sampled repeatedly. Identify the sampling distribution of the sample mean used to build a z interval.
For a normal random sample, the statistic involving (n-1)S squared divided by sigma squared is used to form inference about variance.
The population variance is unknown and replaced by S. Identify why the standardized sample mean follows a t distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom.
A required margin of error is specified for estimating a population mean when sigma is known. Determine the sample size needed for the interval width.
A preliminary sample standard deviation is used to plan a confidence interval width for the population mean.
A two-sided mean interval splits alpha equally between the two tails. Identify the symmetric critical-value construction.

A researcher claims that the mean output exceeds a benchmark. A sample mean is observed, and the question asks how surprising the data would be under the null claim that the mean is no larger than the benchmark.
A one-sided test of H0: mu <= mu0 gives a very small P-value. The question asks for the correct verbal interpretation of that result.
After observing data, a student says the P-value is the probability that H0 is true. Identify the conceptual error in this interpretation.
The wording asks whether the observed statistic is unusually large if the null model is assumed. No fixed accept/reject rule was announced before observing the data.
An experiment reports the extremeness of the observed data under H0 rather than designing a decision rule with specified alpha and beta.

A supplier claims the mean contamination is below a limit. The data produce a low sample mean, and the task is to compute the probability of getting such a low or lower statistic under the boundary null.
The null says the mean equals the target. The observed sample mean is far from the target, and the problem asks for the two-sided extremeness probability.
The problem defines the P-value through outcomes at least as unfavorable to H0 as the one observed, rather than through a pre-fixed critical cutoff.
A manufacturer says a fiber strength is above 150 psi. The data are used to see whether the claim is supported by a small tail probability under H0: mu <= 150.
A technician follows a rule after sampling items and the question asks to describe this rule as a Fisher significance test and compute the P-value of the observed outcome.
Rainfall from treated clouds is sampled to assess whether the mean exceeds a specified acre-feet value. The answer should report a P-value and a conclusion.
The null hypothesis is composite but the P-value is evaluated at the boundary value that makes the observed direction least favorable.
A P-value is reported together with a significance level, and the question asks whether the result would be called significant at that level.
The sample mean is the observed statistic, the null gives its sampling distribution, and the output requested is the tail area beyond the observed value.
The prompt asks what the data say against H0 after the fact; it does not specify a long-run loss constraint before sampling.
Before the data are collected, a test rule must be chosen with alpha fixed at 5%. Then compute the probability of rejection under a specified alternative mean.
Battery life is normal with known sigma. The hypotheses are H0: mu <= 4 and H1: mu >= 4.5. Find the critical region for the sample mean and the power at the alternative.
A decision procedure must reject with probability at least 0.97 when a specified alternative is true. The task is to determine the required sample size.
Repeated noisy reads are averaged to decide between b=0 and b=1. Choose the rejection threshold so that the false alarm probability is 0.01, then control beta.
A batch is accepted or rejected according to a prearranged defective-count cutoff. Choose the cutoff so that the producer's risk is bounded and compute the consumer's risk.
The question emphasizes that the rejection region is fixed before observing the sample, and asks for alpha and power of that rule.
A normal known-sigma test uses a sample mean cutoff. Determine the cutoff that makes the Type I error probability exactly alpha.
Given a proposed critical region, compute the chance of failing to reject H0 when the alternative parameter value is true.
For a one-sided mean test, evaluate the power at a specific non-null mean and explain how it changes with sample size.
A desired power and significance level are fixed. Find the minimum n needed to detect a specified mean shift.
A variance test is designed at alpha=0.01 and the question asks for the power when the true standard deviation is larger than the claimed value.
Two independent samples will be taken with equal size. Choose the sample size to detect a specified difference in means with a required probability of rejection.
Unlike a Fisher test, the alternative value is specified in advance and the test is judged by its long-run rejection probability at that value.
The engineer wants to reduce the chance of rejecting a good process and also asks what this does to the chance of missing a bad process.
A test becomes more stringent by lowering alpha. The problem asks how this affects the critical region and power for the same sample size.
A test statistic falls in the rejection region at the 5% level. State the statistical conclusion without claiming that the alternative has been proven true.
The P-value is larger than alpha. The question asks for the correct conclusion and warns against saying the null hypothesis has been proved.
A manufacturer's claim is to be challenged only if the data are sufficiently inconsistent with it. Decide how the claim should be placed in H0.
The practical claim uses the word exceeds. Determine the alternative direction and the corresponding tail of the test.
The problem asks whether the mean has decreased below a standard. Identify H0, H1, and the left-tailed nature of the decision.
The engineering question is whether the parameter differs from the target in either direction. Choose the two-sided alternative and decision rule.
A very large sample produces a statistically significant but tiny estimated change. The question asks whether this automatically means engineering significance.
Interpret alpha in a fixed decision rule as a long-run probability of rejecting H0 when H0 is true.
Interpret beta for a specified alternative as the probability that the test fails to detect the effect at that alternative.
Given H1: mu > mu0, identify where the rejection region lies for the standardized statistic.
A P-value of 0.047 is obtained and alpha is 0.05. Decide the result while using cautious language about evidence.
The data do not give enough evidence against H0. The problem asks why 'accept H0 as true' is not the correct wording.
For H0: mu <= mu0, the calculation is performed at mu0 to determine the largest tail probability compatible with the null.
A two-sided test at level alpha is compared with a corresponding confidence interval, and the question asks when the null value is rejected.
Different tests give different conclusions on the same data. The question asks for an assumption-based comparison rather than blindly choosing the smaller P-value.
A statement says that P=0.003 means H0 has probability 0.003 of being true. Mark this as incorrect and identify the correct conditional direction.
A student says a significant result at P=0.02 means there is a 2% chance the rejection is a mistake. Identify the flaw.
The question asks what 'or more extreme' means in the definition of a P-value for a right-tailed mean test.
A large P-value is observed. Explain why this is not strong evidence that H0 is exactly true.
A small P-value should be interpreted as data being unusual under the null model, not as direct posterior probability.
For a composite one-sided null, identify why the P-value is evaluated at the least favorable boundary value.
A symmetric test statistic is far from zero. The question asks why both tails enter the P-value.
A P-value is reported after seeing data, while alpha is supposed to define a decision rule before seeing data. Distinguish the two roles.
A two-sided 5% test is compared to whether the 95% confidence interval contains the null value.
The P-value is extremely small, but the estimated effect is negligible in engineering units. Identify the interpretation issue.
A normal measurement process has known sigma. A sample mean from 10 observations is compared to a target, and the question asks whether the true mean is different from the target.
The population standard deviation is known from calibration. The claim is that the mean has increased above the old standard.
The standard deviation is known, and the engineer wants evidence that the mean lifetime has dropped below the guaranteed value.
A small normal sample is summarized by xbar and s. Test whether the population mean exceeds a stated value.
No population sigma is available. A normal sample of 16 values is used to test whether the mean differs from the specification.
The prompt emphasizes that n is small and asks which assumption is needed to use the one-sample mean test with s.
Two possible analyses are proposed for the same sample mean; decide whether the known external sigma or the sample standard deviation determines the statistic.
A one-sample mean problem asks for the P-value of the observed standardized statistic and a final reject/fail-to-reject statement.
A one-sample test gives a statistic just inside the critical boundary. Decide the conclusion at the stated level.
The problem gives only a sample standard deviation but a student wants to use z because the sample is normal. Identify the correct test.
A large sample is taken and sigma is not known. The question asks for the approximate mean test and the role of the CLT.
A small dataset is explicitly assumed normal. The test is about the population mean and uses the sample variance.
Known sigma is available and the question asks how many observations are needed to detect a mean shift with a given power.
The null value for mu is compared with a confidence interval for mu. Decide whether the two-sided test rejects at the matching level.
A manufacturer claims the mean is not more than a threshold, and a sample mean is higher. Choose the appropriate one-sided mean test.
A precision claim concerns the population standard deviation. One normal sample gives s, and the analyst asks whether the true spread exceeds the claim.
The manufacturer claims sigma is not more than a stated value. The sample standard deviation is much larger. Test the claim at alpha=0.01.
The problem asks whether the variance has changed from a historical value in either direction.
A new process is claimed to reduce variability below the old variance. A normal sample variance is used to test this.
A right-tailed chi-square test for standard deviation is designed, and the power is requested at a larger true sigma.
The prompt asks which assumption is crucial when using a chi-square statistic based on sample variance.
A sample of n normal observations is used to test sigma squared. Identify the degrees of freedom in the chi-square statistic.
The sample mean is irrelevant because the claim is about measurement spread. Choose the correct test for sigma.
The problem gives a sample variance and asks both whether a claimed variance is plausible and what range of variances is consistent with the data.
A sample standard deviation is converted into a chi-square statistic and the task is to compute the relevant tail probability for the spread claim.
Two independent normal samples are taken from different locations. The question asks whether their population variances differ.
Line A appears more variable than Line B. Test H0: sigma_A equals sigma_B against the alternative that A is more variable.
The sample variance of the second group is larger, but the alternative is that the first population variance is smaller. Decide how to form the F statistic and tail.
The problem asks whether the F test for equality of variances is appropriate when the two populations are not plausibly normal.
Two sample variances are placed in a ratio. Identify the numerator and denominator degrees of freedom for the chosen ratio.
Before applying a pooled two-sample t procedure, the analyst wants to assess whether equal variances are plausible.
The research question is about whether two suppliers have different variability, not different mean concentration.
An F statistic far from 1 is obtained and the alternative is two-sided. Determine how the P-value should account for both directions.
The sample variances differ, but the problem asks for a formal conclusion at a specified alpha level.
The practical question is whether two standard deviations differ by at least a specified percentage; the setup leads to a variance-ratio decision procedure.
A supplier claims the defective rate is at most 3%. A sample of items contains several defectives, and the buyer wants evidence that the rate is higher.
A campaign claims the success rate is below a benchmark. A large sample provides the number of successes, and a one-sided proportion test is required.
The question is whether a population fraction differs from 0.5 in either direction, based on many independent binary outcomes.
The data are successes and failures. The prompt asks whether the normal approximation for the one-sample proportion test is justified.
A batch is accepted or rejected using the number of defectives in a sample; approximate the binomial decision using a proportion test when appropriate.
Two independent groups have different numbers of successes out of different sample sizes. Test whether their true success probabilities are equal.
Two factories report defective counts out of inspected items. The problem asks whether the defect rates differ.
A new treatment is expected to increase the response probability compared with the standard treatment. Compare two independent proportions.
Under H0: p1=p2, the standard error should use the pooled sample proportion. Identify the procedure.
Estimate the difference between two population proportions using independent binomial samples.
The response is pass/fail, not a continuous measurement. The task asks whether the fraction passing has changed.
The prompt gives very small success counts and asks whether the large-sample z approximation is reliable.
Design a one-sample proportion test with fixed alpha and target power for detecting an increase from p0 to p1.
Each item belongs to exactly one of two independent groups, and each outcome is success or failure. Identify the correct two-sample rate comparison.
The output requested is an interval for the population fraction, not a prediction of how many successes will appear in the next batch.
Two independent groups are measured, and both population standard deviations are known from prior studies. Test whether the new mean is lower than the old mean.
Known equal standard deviations are assumed for two independent samples, and the question asks for sample size to detect a specified difference.
Two independent normal samples are taken and the problem states that population variances may be assumed equal. Compare the means.
Long experience indicates the variability is stable and unaffected by the treatment. Test whether the treatment changes the mean.
Two small independent normal samples have comparable spreads. The task is a two-sample mean test under equal-variance assumption.
The two groups have the same sample size but different physical units; there is no one-to-one matching. Equal variances are assumed.
Two independent samples show clearly different sample variances and unequal sample sizes. Compare the population means without assuming common variance.
The problem explicitly says not to pool the variances when comparing two independent means.
A two-sample t statistic uses s1^2/n1 plus s2^2/n2 in the denominator and an approximate degrees of freedom.
The wording asks whether Student's pooled test or Welch's test is more appropriate when normality is plausible but spreads differ.
A new formulation is expected to reduce drying time. Independent samples under old and new formulations are compared.
Two independent processes are compared to see whether their mean responses differ in either direction.
The population standard deviation for both groups is known and equal. Use this to test and estimate the difference in means.
There is no reason to assume equal variance and sigma is unknown. Identify the safer independent two-mean method.
The problem asks which assumptions are required for independent two-sample t inference with small samples.
The solution requires forming a combined estimate of sigma squared from two independent normal samples under equal-variance assumption.
A replacement catalyst is acceptable only if the mean change is smaller than one standard deviation. Frame the appropriate mean comparison.
Specimens are randomly assigned to formulation 1 or formulation 2. The comparison is between independent means.
The two independent groups have different sample sizes and different sample variances. Select the mean comparison that does not force pooling.
A two-sample mean problem asks for the P-value and a conclusion about the effectiveness of a treatment.
The same subjects are measured under visual and auditory conditions. The question asks whether one condition tends to have a larger mean response.
Each person is measured before and after a training program. Analyze the mean change rather than treating the two columns as independent.
Each specimen is tested once with ball x and once with ball y. Use the within-specimen differences to test expected hardness measurement.
The experiment uses matched pairs to remove specimen-to-specimen variation. The inference target is the mean paired difference.
A small paired dataset has differences that are treated as approximately normal. Test whether the average difference is positive.
For matched observations, construct an interval for the mean difference and decide whether zero is plausible.
The data come in natural pairs; a pooled two-sample test would ignore the dependence structure.
The problem defines D=Y-X and asks whether procedure Y increases the mean response compared with X.
State the assumption needed for the paired t test when the number of pairs is small.
Paired differences are available; compare the parametric paired t approach with a signed-rank alternative.
Only whether each observation is above or below a claimed median is used; magnitudes are intentionally ignored.
A distribution is not assumed normal, and the question asks whether the median exceeds a target using plus and minus signs.
Paired differences are available, and the prompt says their distribution is roughly symmetric but not necessarily normal.
The same units are measured twice, and the question concerns whether the median paired difference is positive.
The procedure ranks absolute paired differences and attaches signs before forming W+ and W-.
Two independent samples are compared for a shift in location without assuming normal distributions.
The samples come from two unrelated groups, and all observations are ranked together to compare typical values.
The response is skewed and sample sizes are small. Compare two independent groups using ranks rather than means.
The problem asks whether to use signed-rank or rank-sum after identifying whether observations are matched.
The prompt asks which nonparametric test discards magnitudes and which one uses ranks of absolute differences.
Observed frequencies for 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 events are compared with frequencies expected under a fitted Poisson model.
One categorical variable gives counts across several bins, and the task is to test whether a proposed probability distribution fits.
Items are classified into four colors. The null says the four category probabilities are equal.
Observed phenotype counts are compared with a theoretical 9:3:3:1 ratio.
A chi-square goodness-of-fit test is performed after estimating one distribution parameter from the same data; determine the degrees of freedom adjustment.
The prompt asks whether expected cell counts are large enough for the chi-square approximation to be trustworthy.
Frequency counts of the number of successes in repeated blocks are compared with a binomial model.
Continuous data are grouped into intervals and compared with expected bin counts under a normal model.
There is only one categorical variable and one set of observed counts. The null specifies category probabilities.
The problem has observed counts across categories but no second classification variable. Select the one-way chi-square procedure.
The expected frequencies depend on a parameter fitted from the sample, so the chi-square degrees of freedom are reduced.
After computing sum (O-E)^2/E over categories, the question asks for the upper-tail P-value.
The final conclusion should state whether the proposed distribution is rejected as a model for the counts.
Some expected counts are too small, and the problem asks whether adjacent categories should be combined before applying the test.
Counts of rare events per unit are tabulated and compared with the pattern predicted by a constant-rate Poisson model.
Digits 0 through 9 are observed from a generator; test whether all ten digits appear with equal probability.
The statistic is built by summing squared standardized differences between observed and expected frequencies over categories.
A contingency table cross-classifies individuals by treatment group and outcome category. Test whether the two classifications are independent.
Defect type is tabulated by production shift. The question asks whether defect type depends on shift.
Survey responses are arranged by gender and preference category. Determine whether preference is independent of gender.
Compute expected counts in each cell using row total times column total divided by grand total.
The data have two categorical variables, each with multiple levels. Choose the chi-square test for association rather than goodness-of-fit.
Several populations are sampled separately and each item is classified into the same response categories; test whether the category distributions are the same.
A table has r rows and c columns. Identify the degrees of freedom for the chi-square independence test.
A contingency table contains cells with small expected counts, and the prompt asks whether the chi-square approximation is acceptable.
After computing the chi-square statistic for a two-way table, state whether there is evidence of association.
The overall table test is significant, and the question asks which cells contribute most through large standardized residuals.
A 2 by 2 table records exposure yes/no and disease yes/no. Test whether exposure and disease status are independent.
A dataset classifies failures by component type and plant location. The task is to decide whether the distributions differ by location.
A poll result is arranged by age group and candidate choice. Test whether candidate choice is independent of age group.
Defect severity categories are cross-tabulated with supplier. Test for dependence between supplier and severity.
The statistic for a contingency table is large; the P-value is the upper tail of a chi-square distribution.
The categories are nominal, and the procedure only uses counts in cells, not numerical distances between category names.
The prompt asks whether a test of independence or a test of homogeneity is the better wording, while the chi-square calculation is the same.
A two-way count table is analyzed, and the answer must mention random sampling and adequate expected counts.
A response is recorded at several values of one quantitative predictor. The task is to estimate beta0 and beta1 by least squares and write the fitted line.
Compressive strength is modeled as a linear function of density. Test whether the slope differs from zero and interpret whether density is useful.
After fitting a simple linear regression, construct a confidence interval for the slope and explain the expected change in response per unit increase in x.
The fitted simple regression output includes the estimated intercept and its standard error. The question asks for a confidence interval for beta0.
After fitting a line, use the residual sum of squares and residual degrees of freedom to estimate sigma squared for the regression errors.
A simple regression ANOVA table gives SSR and SSE. Test whether the regression is significant.
For a simple linear regression with one predictor, compare the t test for beta1=0 with the overall F test.
A large R squared is reported. Identify the correct interpretation as the proportion of observed response variation explained by the fitted model.
A model has R squared close to one. The question asks why this does not prove that the true conditional mean is exactly linear.
Using a fitted regression line, construct an interval for one future response at x0.
Using a fitted line, construct an interval for the average response among all units having predictor value x0.
At the same x0, compare the interval for a new observation with the interval for the mean response and identify which is wider.
A requested prediction uses an x value far outside the range of observed predictor values. Identify the regression risk.
Residuals are plotted against the predictor, and the question asks whether the constant variance assumption appears satisfied.
Residuals show a systematic curved pattern around zero. Decide what this says about the linear mean model.
A scatterplot contains one point with unusual x and large residual. The question asks why this point may heavily affect the fitted line.
The problem asks which assumption justifies t intervals and tests for regression coefficients in a small sample.
Several responses are observed at repeated x values. Test whether the linear model lacks fit by separating pure error from lack-of-fit variation.
Two groups record spring extension at the same mass levels. After fitting a line, test whether the linear relation is adequate using replicated x values.
Two quantitative variables are measured in pairs, and the question asks whether the population correlation is zero.
A sample correlation is reported, and the task is to construct a confidence interval for the population correlation using Fisher's transformation.
A strong sample correlation is observed, and the prompt asks why this alone does not establish a causal effect.
A regression slope P-value is small. State what it says about the data under beta1=0 without claiming the probability beta1 is zero.
A graph shows two bands around a fitted line. Identify which band is for mean response and which is for individual predictions.
The predictor values are fixed by design rather than random. Identify the simple linear regression framework used for Y conditional on x.
A response is modeled using an intercept and several quantitative predictors. Construct the model specification matrix X for least squares.
The task asks for the least-squares estimator beta-hat in matrix form using X transpose X inverse X transpose Y.
The fitted vector is written as HY with H=X(XTX)^(-1)XT. Identify the projection role of the hat matrix.
A multiple regression includes several predictors. Test H0 that all slope coefficients are zero against at least one nonzero slope.
Compare a reduced model and a full model by asking whether the extra predictors significantly reduce SSE.
In a multiple regression output, test whether one predictor has a nonzero coefficient after adjusting for the other predictors.
Construct a confidence interval for a single coefficient in a multiple linear regression model.
A quadratic and an interaction term are added together. The question asks whether this group of terms should remain in the model.
The model includes x and x squared. Test whether the nonlinear term contributes after the linear term is already present.
A qualitative factor is represented by indicator variables in a regression model. Identify the design matrix idea and the coefficient interpretation.
The effect of one predictor may depend on another predictor. Identify the role of an interaction term in the regression model.
Adding predictors cannot decrease ordinary R squared. The prompt asks why adjusted R squared may be used in model comparison.
Two candidate regression models are compared using deleted residual prediction error. Choose the model selection criterion based on PRESS.
A model with many predictors fits the training data very well but may predict poorly. Identify the overfitting issue.
Given a fitted multiple regression and a new predictor vector x0, construct the predicted response and a prediction interval.
For a specified predictor vector x0, estimate the mean response and construct a confidence interval for that mean response.
Use the decomposition of total variation into regression and residual sums of squares in multiple regression.
A multiple regression with p predictors and n observations is fitted. Identify the degrees of freedom for SSR and SSE.
Under the null that all slope coefficients vanish, SSR divided by sigma squared follows a chi-square distribution with p degrees of freedom.
The derivation asks why SSR and SSE are independent under the normal linear model using orthogonal projections.
Residual plots are used after fitting a multiple regression to check linearity, equal variance, and unusual observations.
Two predictors are nearly linear combinations of each other, causing unstable coefficient estimates and large standard errors.
Several nested regression models are proposed. The task is to choose among them using hypothesis tests and prediction-oriented indicators.
R squared is high, but residuals show a clear pattern. Decide why the model may still be inappropriate.
The overall F test is significant, but one individual coefficient t test is not. Interpret this without contradiction.
A reduced model removes two predictors. Test whether removing them causes a significant loss of explanatory power.
For the same predictor vector, distinguish an interval for the conditional mean from an interval for a future observed response.
A data point has an unusual combination of predictor values. The question asks how leverage differs from merely having a large residual.
Interpret a coefficient as the expected change in response for a one-unit change in that predictor while holding other predictors fixed.
A linear model is compared with a model that adds squared terms. Use a partial F test to determine whether the added block is useful.
The requested prediction uses predictor values within the observed design region. Identify the appropriate regression prediction procedure.
A requested prediction uses a combination of predictors far outside the observed data cloud. Identify the extrapolation problem.
Two models have similar R squared but different PRESS values. Identify which one is preferred for prediction.
The question asks whether beta1 plus beta2 equals zero rather than whether either coefficient individually vanishes.
The prompt asks whether a partial F test is appropriate for two proposed models that are not nested.
Predictors are centered before fitting a model with interactions. The question asks how this changes intercept interpretation but not fitted values.
A multiple-choice statement says a large R squared proves the fitted model will make accurate confidence intervals. Identify why that statement is too strong.
A regression test gives a very small P-value. Choose the interpretation in terms of unusual data under the null coefficient restriction.
A slope test fails to reject beta1=0. State the conclusion without saying the predictor has no effect with certainty.
The prompt asks why predictions are less certain farther from the center of the observed x values.
In least squares with an intercept, residuals sum to zero. Identify this as a property of the fitted model, not a data assumption.
The residual standard error is used as the estimate of sigma in the normal error model. Identify where its degrees of freedom come from.
The prompt asks whether the full set of predictors is useful at all, then whether a subset adds value beyond another subset. Identify the two F-test roles.
Replicated predictor settings allow total residual error to be split into pure error and lack-of-fit components.
The model assumes independent errors with mean zero and constant variance around the conditional mean response.
The model with the lowest training SSE is not necessarily best for prediction; deleted residuals are used instead.

A regression output gives coefficient estimates, standard errors, R squared, SSE, and a residual plot. Identify which quantities answer slope significance, explanatory power, variance estimate, and assumption checks.
A group of predictors is removed from a full model, and the question asks whether the reduced model loses significant explanatory ability.
A coefficient is statistically significant in an observational regression. The prompt asks why this alone is not a causal conclusion.
The slope coefficient is interpreted in original engineering units while holding other predictors fixed. Identify the relevant regression coefficient interpretation.
